content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 26, 2016

Ivy M. Tsai
Assistant City Attorney
City of West Covina
Jones & Mayer
3777 N. Harbor Blvd
Fullerton, CA 92835

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-15-241

Dear Ms. Tsai:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the City of West Covina
regarding the campaign finance provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ The Fair
Political Practices Commission is not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. Please
note that this advice relates solely to the Act and does not address the applicability of laws outside
our purview, including, but not limited to, the provisions relating to voter-requested recounts
contained in Elections Code Section 15620 et seq. and the prohibition against the misuse of public
funds set forth in Penal Code Section 425.

QUESTION

If the City adopts an ordinance authorizing it to reimburse the costs of a recount in an
election for a City office in which the margin of victory between two candidates is less than one-
half of one percent of the ballots cast after completion of the official canvass, would a
reimbursement payment made pursuant to the ordinance be a "contribution" under the Act?

CONCLUSION

No. A reimbursement payment made pursuant to the proposed ordinance would not be a
"contribution" under the Act because it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that such a
payment is not made for political purposes.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-15-241
Page No. 2

FACTS

In some past elections for seats on the West Covina City Council, the vote counts for the
two candidates for a single seat have been extremely close, sometimes within a few votes. Past
recounts of City election results, in elections consolidated with the County of Los Angeles, have
demonstrated multiple counting errors which could have affected the results of the elections. The
City is currently unable to control the accuracy of vote counts for local elections because the
counting of ballots and votes is conducted by the County of Los Angeles. The potential cost of a
recount in a local election is substantial. The City argues that this potential cost discourages
candidates from running for local office and voters from requesting a recount to ensure the voters'
will is carried out.

You are an Assistant City Attorney for the City of West Covina. The City is currently
considering enacting an ordinance that would authorize the City to reimburse the costs of a recount
in an election for a City office where the margin of victory is less than one-half of one percent of
the ballots cast.

ANALYSIS

The term "contribution" is defined by Section 82015 and Regulation 18215 to include "any
payment made for political purposes." Section 82015(a) provides as follows:

"(a) 'Contribution' means a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a payment
of a loan by a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment except
to the extent that full and adequate consideration is received, unless it is clear
from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes."

Regulation 18215 further defines "contribution" for purposes of the Act, specifies when a
payment is "made for political purposes," and provides in pertinent part as follows:

"(a) A contribution is any payment made for political purposes for which a
donor does not receive full and adequate consideration. A payment is made for
political purposes if it is:
(1) For the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of
the voters for or against the nomination or election of a candidate or
candidates, or the qualification or passage of any measure; or
(2) Received by or made at the behest of the following or any agent
thereof:
(A) A candidate;
(B) A controlled committee;
(C) An official committee of a political party, including a state central
committee, county central committee, assembly district committee, or any
subcommittee of such committee; or
(D) An organization formed or existing primarily for political purposes,
including, but not limited to, a political action committee established by any
membership organization, labor union, or corporation."

File No. A-15-241
Page No. 3

Therefore, to determine whether a reimbursement payment made pursuant to the proposed ordinance would be a "contribution" under the Act, we must first determine if it would be "made for political purposes" either because it was for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against a candidate, or because it was received by or made at the behest of a candidate, a controlled committee, or an official committee of a political party.

A reimbursement payment made pursuant to the ordinance would not be "for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of voters" for or against a candidate because there is no further "action of the voters" to influence as a recount cannot be requested until after completion of the official canvass under Section 2-602 of the proposed ordinance. Accordingly, we find that the ordinance would not be for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against a candidate.

We note that the express purposes of the proposed ordinance are to encourage residents to run for City offices and to minimize the risk that vote counting errors will go uncorrected (Proposed Section 2-600, West Covina Municipal Code), and that these are not inherently political purposes. We also note that the proposed ordinance expressly provides that "[a]ctions taken by the City Council and payments made pursuant to this ordinance shall not be considered a contribution to any candidate who may be involved in a vote recount authorized by the City Council." (Ibid.)

Under the election law procedure, the recount might be requested by a voter on behalf of a candidate. Here, however, where the payment is made by the City pursuant to a local ordinance providing for reimbursement of vote recounts in close local elections, it is not considered a payment for political purposes. The payment is part of the vote recount process set forth by local ordinance, and this advice assumes that the City Council would authorize recounts in all elections where the margin of victory between two candidates is less than one-half of one percent of the ballots cast, if requested. Under these facts, even if a reimbursement payment under the proposed ordinance were made for a recount requested by a voter on behalf of a candidate, it would not be considered a "contribution" under the Act.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

payment is not made for political purposes.
